text: The present flowsheet for processing a single-fluid MSBR includes a fluorination step for continuously removing $99\%$ of the uranium from a salt stream coming from the reactor at the rate of 3.3 liters/min.

Operability of a frozen wall fluorinator using autoresistance power to simulate fission product heating has been tested in three experiments: AHT-2, AHT-3, and AHT-4.1-4 In experiment AHT-2, LiF-BeF $_2$ salt (67-33 mole %) was used in a simple, vertical test vessel with satisfactory results.2 In AHT-3, successful runs were made using MSBR fuel carrier salt with the electrode in the vertical test section.5 In AHT-4, MSBR
The objective of the continuous fluorinator experimental facility (CFEF) is to demonstrate the actual fluorination of uranium in a circulating salt system that is similar to AHT-4.
The continuous fluorinator experimental facility will be installed in a cell in Building 7503 to provide beryllium containment.

The system will contain 8 to $9\mathrm{ft}^3$ (0.23 to $0.25\mathrm{m}^3$ ) of MSBR fuel-carrier salt (72-16-12 mole $\%$ LiF-BeF $_2$ -ThF $_4$ ) containing an initial quantity of 0.35 mole $\%$ of uranium.
The salt will be circulated at up to $100\%$ of MSBR flow rate (3.3 liters/min).
Because of the short fluorination height and depending on operating conditions, the uranium that is volatilized will range between $80\%$ and $95\%$ .

The surge tank is designed to provide sufficient salt inventory for about 4 hr of fluorination under operating conditions which result in $80\%$ uranium volatilization per pass, and 11 hr of fluorination under conditions which result in $95\%$ uranium volatilization per pass.
About $99\%$ of the uranium should have been removed from the salt batch after these periods of time.
The surge tank will have a working volume of $5\mathrm{ft}^3$ ( $0.14\mathrm{m}^3$ ), and a dip tube through which hydrogen can be introduced if further uranium reduction is required.
Off-gas from the fluorinator will contain $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{6}$ and excess fluorine; argon will also be in the off-gas if it is used to dilute the fluorine.
The difference between the two readings is a measure of the $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{6}$ flow rate.
An engineering layout of the equipment to be installed in the spare equipment cell in Building 7503 is shown in ref.
2) is similar to the test vessel used in experiments AHT-3 and AHT-4; however, several changes have been made based on operating experience.
The diameter of the entire vessel was increased from 6 to 8 in.
(0.15 to $0.20\mathrm{m}$ ) to simplify frozen-film formation and to provide space for a thicker film, thus minimizing the effect of nonuniform film thickness.
Fluorination zones of 3 and 6 ft (0.9 and $1.8\mathrm{m}$ ) are provided by having two fluorine inlets.

Since complete fluorination is not expected, and $\mathrm{UF}_5$ (which is very corrosive) will be present in the salt below the fluorine inlet, it will be necessary to cool all five jackets regardless of whether the 3-ft or 6-ft (0.9 or $1.8\mathrm{m}$ ) fluorinator is being used.

Although corrosion would be severe (probably $\sim 0.03 \, \text{mm/hr}$ ), at least $100 \, \text{hr}$ of fluorination should be possible before the jacket fails and/or the salt is saturated with $\text{NiF}_2$ ( $\sim 0.9 \, \text{wt\%}$ ).

Figure 3 shows, for example, that the heat loss through a 1-in.- (25-mm) thick salt film could be compensated by a salt temperature drop of $40^{\circ} \text{C}$ (inlet-exit) at one-half the MSBR salt flow rate if a $400^{\circ} \text{C}-$ pipe wall temperature is desired.
The salt head in the fluorinator will determine the gas-lift submergence (about $50\%$ ), and the salt circulation rate (1 to 3 liters/min) will determine the column diameter.
Horizontal lines show heat removal from salt flowing through the fluorinator at 1.7 liters per minute [1/2 the processing rate for a 1000-MW(e) MSBR] for the indicated salt temperature change.
Before being returned to the fluorinator, the salt will pass through a $5\text{-ft}^3$ $(0.14\text{-m}^3)$ surge tank to provide salt inventory.
The bed depth is 3.3 ft (1.0 m), and has a calculated pressure drop of 0.15 psi (1034 kPa) with a gas flow rate of 1 cfm (4.7 x $10^{-4}$ m³/sec).

The bed has the capacity to absorb the HF evolved from 1.3-fuel salt batches containing 0.35 mole % uranium, assuming no $\mathsf{UF}_6$ evolution (all $\mathsf{UF}_4$ is converted to $\mathsf{UF}_5$ , and is reduced by hydrogen to $\mathsf{UF}_4$ ).
Since absorption is poor at high temperatures, the gas from the reduction step is cooled to $100^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ before it enters the trap.

Cooling the gas to below $100^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ at the inlet would result in a high partial pressure of HF (probably $\sim 20\%$ ), which would form the higher HF complexes (NaF·2HF, NaF·3HF, etc.) and cause plugging.
One of these absorbers, having a bed depth of 10 in.
(0.25 m), contains 24 kg of NaF and has a capacity of about 15 kg of uranium.
Less than 10 kg of uranium is contained in 8 ft³ (0.23 m³) of fuel salt.
The absorbers are designed with an open 2-in.
(51-mm) center pipe for air
In order to minimize the equipment operating at the autoresistance potential (up to 200 V), a pot will be inserted between the heat flowmeter and the electrode-salt inlet pipe in the fluorinator.
1), the pot and fluorinator both have insulated flanges so that only the pot and fluorinator top flange will be at high potential.
The salt leaving the surge tank will pass through a 28-in.
(0.7l-m) section of 2-in. nickel pipe containing a cartridge heater.
4) has provisions for the connection of two $18\text{-std-m}^3$ fluorine trailers with safety controls to limit the maximum flow rate and to remotely stop the flow in case of a leak.
A vertical scrubber is being installed in Building 7503 for the disposal of the excess fluorine.
The scrubber is a 6-in., 8-ft- (2.4-m) high Monel pipe with three spray nozzles in the upper half of the vessel.
The surge tank contains 200 gal $(0.76\mathrm{m}^3)$ of an aqueous solution containing 15 wt % KOH and 5 wt % KI.
This equipment is designed to be able to dispose of one trailer of fluorine (18 std m³) at a flow rate of 30 slm.
The KOH solution will be circulated through the spray nozzles at a maximum total flow rate of 15 gpm (9.5 x $10^{-4}\mathrm{m}^3/\mathrm{sec}$ ).
After the system has been leak tested, all salt-containing equipment and piping will be purged with argon and heated to $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Approximately 65 liters will overflow through the flowmeter and circuit-breaking pot to the fluorinator.
After adjusting the salt inventory, salt circulation will be started again, and temperatures in the fluorinator will be adjusted to $530^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Lines and other equipment can be maintained between $530^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The autoresistance power will be turned on at very low voltage (less than 1 V) and the resistance can be calculated periodically from the current measurement as the cooling proceeds.
When all pipe wall temperatures are less than $350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the resistance of the salt from the electrode to the bottom of the fluorinator will have increased considerably.
